title: Research
text:
I am interested in how species adapt to their environment, and how they might respond to environmental disturbances e.g., climate change, harvesting, or other anthropogenic pressures.
I would like to develop my skills in quantitative genetics, temporal genomics, and ecophysiology. I am particularly interested in applying genomic tools to the study of endangered species, and to inform sustainable fisheries management. I am also interested in projects aimed at capacity building (in terms of the application of genetics/genomics to conservation) in East Africa. Please reach out if you might have opportunities to collaborate in this space in the future (I will be submitting my PhD in mid-2027)…

title: Research
text:
Coral population & conservation genomics
My PhD thesis thesis employs population and evolutionary genomics to uncover cryptic diversity in reef corals and to understand the processes that generate and maintain it, including speciation, gene flow, reproductive isolation and local adaptation.
My thesis centres on the brooding coral Seriatopora hystrix along the Great Barrier Reef, where genomic data reveal cryptic species and habitat specialisation: lineages that look alike but are genetically distinct and sorted across reef environments.
Alongside this species-level work I take a macrogenetic view, synthesising and comparing genetic diversity across many coral species and across space to explore what general factors shape diversity on reefs. Related projects include estimates of genetic diversity and differentiation in the Caribbean coral Agaricia tenuifolia, and causal modelling showing that corals in more environmentally heterogeneous reefs can carry lower genetic diversity. Together this work supports efforts such as the Reef Restoration and Adaptation Program to conserve and restore corals in a warming ocean.




Seriatopora hystrix colony in the field





Close-up of Seriatopora hystrix branches





Branching Acropora coral

title: Research
text:
Environmental DNA & biodiversity monitoring
Traces of DNA shed into seawater and sediments can reveal the species present on a reef without ever seeing them. I use environmental DNA (eDNA) and DNA metabarcoding to detect and monitor biodiversity, approaches that can make surveys faster, less invasive and more sensitive to hard-to-observe life stages. My Honours research utilised metabarcoding to characterise the spatio-temporal distribution of planktonic echinoderm larvae, and I have since applied eDNA to monitor benthic communities in Quandamooka (Moreton Bay).
I have also contributed to reef community monitoring through Reef Check Australia surveys and the Mooloolaba Ecological Assessment and Mapping project.

title: Research
text:
Beyond marine systems
While my work so far has focused on marine systems, the genomic and analytical approaches I use apply broadly across taxa, and I am always keen to extend them to new systems, questions and collaborations.






For a full list of talks, posters and publications, see Publications & Talks.

title: Ilha Byrne

Ilha Byrne

title: About
text:
My PhD research looks at patterns of spatial genetic variation in corals on the Great Barrier Reef and in Honduras. My PhD is primarily funded by the Reef Restoration and Adaptation Program, the Paul M. Angell Foundation, and a Holsworth Research Endowment. I am supported by an ELEVATE Boosting Women in STEM Scholarship and was a Global Change Scholar with UQ and CSIRO (2023-2024).
Before moving to Australia for my undergraduate studies in 2017, I trained and worked as a PADI Divemaster in Tanzania (which is where I grew up). I went on to study at USyd and UQ, completing a First-Class Honours project on using DNA metabarcoding to characterise the distribution of planktonic echinoderm larvae, and graduating with the University Medal as Faculty Graduate of the Year.
Following my undergraduate studies, I worked as a full-time research assistant with the Riginos Research Group (2021-2023), primarily leading eDNA sample collection and laboratory processing, bioinformatics, and stakeholder engagement for the Sustainable Urban Seascapes Moreton Bay (Quandamooka) project. I also collaborated with and led laboratory assays for researchers in the Marine Spatial Ecology Lab, the Tibbetts Lab and Dr. Ordonez Alvarez at USC. My work combines population and conservation genomics, evolutionary genomics and environmental DNA to connect patterns of genetic variation to the environments and evolutionary processes that produce them.
I am an active science communicator and a long-standing volunteer with Reef Check Australia, working to bring science to schools, community groups and the public.




Fieldwork at Woppaburra (the Keppel Islands)





Reef flat at Heron Island, Great Barrier Reef

title: Teaching & Outreach
text: Sharing marine science with students, communities and the public is a passion of mine. Alongside my research I teach at university, communicate science online and in classrooms, and volunteer with various conservation NGOs.

title: Teaching & Outreach
text:
Teaching
I have tutored and demonstrated undergraduate courses at the University of Queensland since 2018, and teach into international study-abroad programs.

International Programs Tutor, University of Queensland: University of California and Stanford University Study Abroad Programs.
Demonstrator: Biostatistics & Experimental Design (BIOL2006), Environment & Society (GEOS1100), Zoology (BIOL2204) and Marine Science (MARS1001).
Peer-Assisted Study Session & Science Learning Centre Tutor: university-funded academic tutoring and mentoring in biology, genetics, chemistry and statistics.

title: Teaching & Outreach
text:
Science communication & outreach

Science communication: I share marine science and life as a coral researcher on Instagram @biologist.byrne.
Wonder of Science: led and supported STEM activities for school students across Queensland, including in regional and remote communities (an initiative of the Queensland Chief Scientist).
Curious Minds Coach: mentored high-school students who are aspiring women in STEM.
Reef Check Australia Team Leader & Ambassador: represented reef science and conservation at markets, festivals and community events, and coordinated the Coast to Coral online seminar series.
UQ science engagement: developed and delivered in-classroom and outdoor STEM experiences for high-school students, and university STEM and recruitment events.

title: Teaching & Outreach
